title: No Internet Connection
cardText: Diagnose devices that cannot access websites, online services, or cloud applications.
Determine whether the problem is Wi-Fi, Ethernet, DNS, router, ISP, VPN, or device related.
Separate local device issues from network-wide outages.
text: This guide is for devices that cannot access the internet.
Determine whether the issue affects one device or multiple devices.
Check local connectivity before restarting network equipment.
Escalate ISP or infrastructure issues when local troubleshooting fails.

checkAffectedDevices.title: Check whether other devices have internet access
body: Test another phone, tablet, laptop, or computer on the same network.
This helps determine whether the issue is isolated to one device or affects the entire network.
Knowing this early prevents unnecessary troubleshooting.

checkConnectionType.title: Identify connection type
body: Determine whether the affected device is using Wi-Fi or Ethernet.
The troubleshooting path differs depending on how the device connects to the network.

checkWifiConnection.title: Check Wi-Fi connection status
body: Verify the device is connected to the correct wireless network.
Check whether the Wi-Fi icon shows Connected, Connected without Internet, or Disconnected.
Reconnect if necessary.

reconnectWifi.title: Reconnect to Wi-Fi
body: Connect to the correct wireless network.
Verify the Wi-Fi password is correct.
Once connected, test internet access again.

checkEthernetCable.title: Check Ethernet cable
body: Ensure the Ethernet cable is firmly connected at both ends.
Look for link lights on the network port if available.
Try another cable if possible.

replaceEthernetCable.title: Replace Ethernet cable
body: A damaged cable can prevent network connectivity.
Replace the cable and retest the connection.

testWebsiteAccess.title: Test access to websites
body: Open a web browser and try visiting several different websites.
Do not rely on a single website because the site itself may be offline.
Test both familiar and well-known public websites.

dnsIssueCheck.title: Check for DNS issues
body: If some websites work but others do not, DNS resolution may be failing.
DNS translates website names into IP addresses.
A DNS problem can make the internet appear partially broken.

changeDnsServer.title: Change DNS server
body: Try using a reliable public DNS provider such as Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) or Google DNS (8.8.8.8).
Flush DNS cache if appropriate.
Retest website access after making changes.

checkNetworkAdapter.title: Check network adapter status
body: Open network settings and confirm the network adapter is enabled.
Disable and re-enable the adapter if necessary.
A disabled adapter can prevent internet access even when hardware appears normal.

enableAdapter.title: Enable network adapter
body: The network adapter was disabled.
Enable it and test the internet connection again.

checkIpAddress.title: Check IP address assignment
body: Verify the device has received an IP address from the router.
A device without an IP address cannot communicate properly on the network.
Look for messages such as Limited Connectivity or Self-Assigned Address.

renewIpAddress.title: Renew IP address
body: Disconnect and reconnect to the network.
Restart the device or renew the IP address lease.
If the problem persists, investigate router DHCP settings.

checkVpnUsage.title: Check for VPN usage
body: Determine whether a VPN is active.
A misconfigured or disconnected VPN can block internet access.
Temporarily disconnect the VPN and test again.

disableVpn.title: Disable VPN temporarily
body: Disconnect the VPN connection.
Test websites and online services again.
If internet access returns, review VPN settings.

restartDevice.title: Restart the device
body: Restart the affected device.
Temporary network stack problems can sometimes be resolved by a reboot.
Retest connectivity after startup.

resetNetworkSettings.title: Reset network settings
body: Reset network settings if available.
This can remove corrupted configuration and restore default networking behaviour.
Reconnect to the network afterwards.

deviceNetworkEscalation.title: Further device diagnostics required
body: The issue appears isolated to one device.
Investigate network drivers, operating system corruption, security software, or hardware faults.
Escalate if the device still cannot connect.

checkRouterStatus.title: Check router and modem status lights
body: Inspect the router and modem.
Look for warning lights, flashing fault indicators, or missing internet lights.
Compare the lights with the provider's documentation if available.

restartRouter.title: Restart modem and router
body: Power off the modem and router.
Wait at least 30 seconds.
Power them back on and wait for all status lights to stabilize.

checkIspOutage.title: Check ISP outage information
body: Check the internet provider's website, service status page, mobile app, or recorded support line.
Look for known outages affecting your area.
Many internet problems are caused by provider-side faults.

waitForIspRepair.title: Wait for ISP restoration
body: The provider has reported an outage.
Monitor the outage status and wait for service restoration.
No local troubleshooting is likely to resolve the issue until the outage is fixed.

checkDirectConnection.title: Check direct modem connection
body: If appropriate, connect a device directly to the modem.
This helps determine whether the router or modem is responsible.
Only perform this if you understand the network layout.

routerFaultLikely.title: Router fault likely
body: Internet works when bypassing the router.
The router configuration or hardware may be faulty.
Review router settings or replace the router if necessary.

contactIsp.title: Contact ISP
body: Local troubleshooting has not restored internet access.
Provide the ISP with details of the tests already completed.
Request line testing and confirmation of service status.
